title: Variationally Compressing Quantum Circuits to Approximate Nonadiabatic Molecular Quantum Dynamics
authors: Joshua M. Courtney, P. C. Stancil
abstract: Quantum simulation has begun to penetrate the field of quantum chemistry in hopes of efficiently calculating ground state energies and approximating real-time evolution. With modern research highlighting nonadiabatic dynamics, tunably approximating deep circuits representing potential landscapes becomes crucial for simulating real quantum systems. Variationally approximating unitaries allows for shallower circuits and accuracy tunable to hardware fidelity, so long as the observable quantities are preserved. We show the variational compression of Trotter terms preserve reaction rate coefficients via classical emulation of a hybrid quantum-classical optimization method, as well as fast-forwarded adiabatic dynamics on quantum hardware. Compressed circuits can be incorporated with product-formula-based time evolution to approximate dynamics of a particle in two coupled harmonic potentials, allowing tunability when removing high-cost qubit interactions. Approximate rate coefficients are recovered after substituting terms in a nonadiabatic dynamic process, giving proof-of-principle for observable preservation under variational optimization. Attention is paid to minimizing qubit and gate-count resources.
summary: This paper presents a method to compress quantum circuits used for simulating nonadiabatic molecular dynamics by truncating long-range interactions in a Walsh expansion. By using a variational approach, the authors show that they can significantly reduce gate counts and depth while preserving essential physical observables like reaction rates. The method was successfully validated through both classical emulation and experiments on IBM quantum processors.
main_problem: The high circuit depth and gate overhead required to simulate nonadiabatic molecular dynamics on NISQ and early fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
main_result: Demonstrated that variational compression of Trotter terms using l-local truncation preserves reaction rate coefficients and allows for 'fast-forwarding' dynamics with reduced gate counts and depth.
method: Variational compression using a Walsh function expansion and a Variational Fast-Forwarding (VFF) ansatz, optimized via Quantum-Assisted Quantum Compiling (QAQC) with the Local Hilbert-Schmidt Test.
model_or_system: The Marcus model for nonadiabatic electron transfer, involving a particle in two coupled harmonic potentials and a position-dependent coupling function, implemented on IBM Brisbane, Torino, and Sherbrooke quantum processors.
key_observables: Reaction rate coefficients, electronic state populations, entanglement fidelity, and state fidelity.
important_parameters: Number of qubits (n), locality parameter (l), driving force (delta G), reorganization energy (lambda), and temperature (T).
main_assumptions: The approximation assumes that quadratic operators can be effectively represented by truncated Walsh series and that hardware gate errors can be matched to logical approximation errors.
figures_summary: Comparison of explicit vs. compressed circuit decompositions; plots of QAQC optimization convergence; hardware validation showing state fidelity vs. timesteps; and comparison of extracted Marcus model rate coefficients against theoretical values.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of circuit depth in molecular dynamics, presents the variational compression and VFF methodology, details the QAQC optimization protocol, provides classical emulation results, and concludes with experimental validation on IBM quantum hardware.
why_interesting: It provides a scalable strategy for simulating complex time-dependent dynamics by trading off precision for circuit shallowing, which is directly applicable to simulating many-body dynamics and open quantum systems on near-term hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Explicit circuit decomposition for the same quadratic operator on n = 3 qubits. Operations are ordered by poly- nomial degree: global phase (ϕ1), linear terms (ϕ2), and quadratic terms (ϕ3). The quadratic terms require all-to- all connectivity between qubits. (above) Quadratic circuit as presented by Benenti and Strini [59] and Ollitrault et al. [23]. (below) Tensor product commutativity reduces resources with an equivalently expressive circuit.

FIG. 2. Quantum-assisted quantum compiling (QAQC) cir- cuit for measuring entanglement fidelity. Bell pairs are pre- pared between corresponding qubits in registers A and B. Bell basis measurement extracts entanglement fidelity F (j) e for each qubit pair, requiring measurement of all n pairs of qubits.
FIG. 3. Marcus model for nonadiabatic dynamics with a linearly-approximated Gaussian coupling. Two diabatic po- tential energy surfaces V0 and V1 are offset by ∆rG0.

FIG. 4. Ansatz structure for l = 3-local truncation on n = 8 qubits. Gates are organized by locality: l = 1 (single- qubit Rz), l = 2 (nearest-neighbor ZZ), and l = 3 (next- nearest-neighbor ZZ). The structure W follows the first three sublayers of D, truncated at l = 2-local ZZ terms.

FIG. 5. First-order Trotter step circuit for nonadiabatic dynamics. The position register (n qubits) encodes the wavepacket; the objective qubit stores diabatic state populations; ancilla and comparator qubits support the coupling operation. The approximated kinetic operator T ′ is sandwiched between centered quantum Fourier transforms (cQFT). Approximated potential operators V ′ 0 and V ′ 1 are conditioned on the objective qubit state.
